title: Header interfaces and include guards

canonicalExplanation.summary: A C module boundary places stable public declarations and documented contracts in a header and keeps private helpers and representations inside source files.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A stack header declares an incomplete struct Stack and public functions while stack.c defines representation and private resize helpers. Your goal is to create a self-contained header and matching implementation for a small module. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Failure-safe cleanup; Scope, linkage and storage duration; Declarations, prototypes and definitions; Type aliases and API clarity. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A C module boundary places stable public declarations and documented contracts in a header and keeps private helpers and representations inside source files. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: C has no standard module privacy for ordinary headers, exposed structure definitions freeze layout, and circular includes signal poorly separated dependencies. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Every function and structure definition should be placed in a header so all source files can share it.

includes: Separate public declarations from private definitions without duplicate-definition hazards.
Create a self-contained header and matching implementation for a small module.
excludes: C has no standard module privacy for ordinary headers, exposed structure definitions freeze layout, and circular includes signal poorly separated dependencies.

outcomes: Create a self-contained header and matching implementation for a small module.

examples.title: Header interfaces and include guards: defined case
examples.situation: A stack header declares an incomplete struct Stack and public functions while stack.c defines representation and private resize helpers.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A stack header declares an incomplete struct Stack and public functions while stack.c defines representation and private resize helpers.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Expose only required declarations and contracts in headers; keep ordinary external definitions and private implementation details in source files.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Expose only required declarations and contracts in headers; keep ordinary external definitions and private implementation details in source files.

examples.title: Header interfaces and include guards: boundary case
examples.situation: Headers include only dependencies needed for their own declarations and use guards so inclusion order does not alter validity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Headers include only dependencies needed for their own declarations and use guards so inclusion order does not alter validity.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: C has no standard module privacy for ordinary headers, exposed structure definitions freeze layout, and circular includes signal poorly separated dependencies.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Every function and structure definition should be placed in a header so all source files can share it.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Create a self-contained header and matching implementation for a small module.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every function and structure definition should be placed in a header so all source files can share it.
misconceptions.correction: Expose only required declarations and contracts in headers; keep ordinary external definitions and private implementation details in source files.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Every function and structure definition should be placed in a header so all source files can share it.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Header interfaces and include guards: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
C has no standard module privacy for ordinary headers, exposed structure definitions freeze layout, and circular includes signal poorly separated dependencies.
